title: Cerebras and the re-weighting of the AI compute stack
question: A GPU AI factory makes money from fragmentation - splitting models across thousands of reticle-limited dies and re-stitching them with HBM, CoWoS, NVLink and optical networking (~a third of the bill of materials). Cerebras internalizes that re-stitching onto one 46,225 mm2 wafer with 44 GB of on-die SRAM and no HBM. If wafer-scale wins share, how does the semiconductor value chain re-weight - who benefits, who is exposed, and how much share can it actually take before the CUDA moat, the SRAM ceiling and the incumbent close the window?
finding: The technology is real and the mechanism is the story: wafer-scale genuinely removes the memory-and-interconnect re-stitching layer (HBM-per-accelerator, CoWoS, NVLink/NVSwitch, most scale-out networking), relocating memory to an external commodity-DRAM tier (MemoryX) plus a thin fabric (SwarmX). But it is a SPIKE, not a platform: the CUDA moat (~5M developers) and the 44 GB on-wafer SRAM ceiling confine the durable win to low-latency / agentic inference - the realistic ceiling is the 'AMD/Arm of fast inference,' a durable #2/#3 owning a sub-segment, not a CUDA-killer. The most investable idea is the second-order value-chain RE-WEIGHT, which no sell-side model captures yet: if wafer-scale takes share, pain concentrates in networking (Arista ~29% of rev is AI back-end Ethernet), optics (Coherent ~75% datacom), HBM (SK Hynix - HBM ~45% of a B200's COGS, Cerebras uses none) and CoWoS packaging (ASE/Amkor), while TSMC raw wafers (a full 5nm wafer per part) and power/cooling (Vertiv, 23 kW liquid-cooled) benefit. Almost none of this bites in 2026-2027 (Cerebras is a rounding error vs Nvidia's ~800k CoWoS wafers/yr); it is a 2027-2030 structural watch that shows first as slower-than-consensus growth, not absolute declines. Scenarios: bear/limited ~30%, base/niche ~50%, bull/major-disruption ~20% - only the base-plus and bull paths make the supply-chain trade actionable. Cohort context: Cerebras is the ONLY listable pure-play among inference-ASIC challengers, which split by memory - SRAM/no-HBM (Cerebras, Groq, d-Matrix) actually drives the HBM-displacement thesis, vs an HBM camp (SambaNova, Rebellions, Etched) that feeds the stack. Nvidia's ~$20bn license + acqui-hire of Groq (Dec 2025, not closed, under antitrust review) is the clearest evidence the incumbent is absorbing the niche, and leaves CBRS the last independent listable SRAM pure-play (Groq->Nvidia, Tenstorrent->Qualcomm talks, SambaNova->Intel stake, Graphcore->SoftBank). CBRS the stock is a concentrated, capital-intensive execution bet: IPO'd at $185 (2026-05-14, ~$56.4bn fully-diluted), ~$510M 2025 revenue (profitable) but ~86% from two UAE-linked entities (G42), and a single-source supply chain that must scale ~10x. The call lives or dies on customer diversification (top-2 below ~50%), gross-margin/RPO conversion, and whether a Western hyperscaler ever adopts wafer-scale internally - not on the chip.
